title: SEGMENT 11 — Functional metagenomics: the discovery engine

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — Functional metagenomics: the discovery engine
So far we've been talking about metagenomics mostly as a tool for cataloging — figuring out what's where, in the gut, in the ocean, in the soil.
But metagenomics has another use that may, in the long run, be more important: it's a discovery engine for new biology.

Every new microbial genome contains genes that nobody has seen before, encoding enzymes that do chemistry nobody has characterized, sometimes producing molecules with useful biological activity that could be turned into drugs, industrial catalysts, or new molecular tools.
Functional metagenomics is the practice of mining environmental DNA for these novel functions.
Two of the biggest hits in modern molecular biology came out of, or were dramatically expanded by, functional metagenomics.
The first is CRISPR-Cas systems, the now-famous bacterial defense systems that have been turned into genome-editing tools.
The original CRISPR-Cas9 system, discovered in Streptococcus pyogenes and developed for editing by Jennifer Doudna, Emmanuelle Charpentier, Feng Zhang, and others, was just the beginning.
Subsequent surveys of environmental microbial genomes — both cultured and metagenome-derived — have identified an entire zoo of CRISPR systems with different properties.

CRISPR-Cas12a (also called Cpf1) was identified by Eugene Koonin's computational analysis of microbial genomic data and developed into a different kind of editing tool with distinct PAM requirements and cut patterns.

CRISPR-Cas13, which targets RNA rather than DNA, was identified by Koonin's group from metagenomic data and turned into a programmable RNA-targeting system useful for RNA editing and diagnostics — the SHERLOCK diagnostic platform is built on Cas13.
The CasX system was identified from groundwater metagenomes by Jill Banfield's group at Berkeley.
Functional metagenomics has, essentially, kept the CRISPR toolbox expanding for a decade.
The second major area is natural products and antibiotics.

The classical way to discover new antibiotics was to grow soil bacteria — mostly Streptomyces species, as we discussed in the antibiotics lecture — and screen their fermentation broths for compounds that killed test bacteria.
This worked beautifully for a few decades but ran into the rediscovery problem: the same compounds kept showing up over and over, because the same culturable soil bacteria kept being isolated.

Functional metagenomics offered an alternative: clone DNA fragments from environmental samples directly into a heterologous host like E. coli or Streptomyces lividans, induce expression, and screen for activity.
If a fragment encodes a complete biosynthetic gene cluster for a small molecule, the host might produce that molecule and you can detect it.
Various academic and industrial groups have used this approach to recover novel antibiotic candidates from soil DNA that came from organisms nobody had ever cultured.

A related approach is bioinformatic mining: take all the sequenced microbial genomes and metagenomes, scan them computationally for biosynthetic gene clusters (using tools like antiSMASH), and prioritize clusters that look novel for laboratory follow-up.
The Sean Brady lab at Rockefeller, the Bradley Moore lab at Scripps, and many others have used this approach to identify and characterize new natural product classes.

The discovery of teixobactin, a new antibiotic class identified by Kim Lewis at Northeastern in 2015 using a clever culturing device called the iChip that grows previously uncultivable bacteria in situ in soil, is a related triumph — not quite metagenomics, but in the same spirit of accessing the previously inaccessible majority.
The same logic extends to other classes of useful molecules.

Novel enzymes for industrial catalysis — proteases, lipases, cellulases for biofuel production — are routinely identified from environmental metagenomes, particularly from extreme environments like hot springs (where the enzymes are thermostable, useful for industrial processes) and deep-sea sediments.
Novel cold-adapted enzymes from polar metagenomes are useful in food processing.

Some pharmaceutical companies and biotechs have substantial pipelines built around metagenomic enzyme discovery, with players like Ginkgo Bioworks and Zymergen (which had a notable corporate collapse, but the underlying technology persists) building entire businesses around it.
Here is the analogy.

Functional metagenomics is like turning the world's environmental DNA into a global library of unread books, then training computers to skim the books for plots and characters you've never seen before, and finally pulling the most interesting ones off the shelf to actually read them in a lab.
The library is enormous — every gram of soil, every milliliter of seawater is a new collection — and most of it has never been opened.
The tools to read it — both wet-lab heterologous expression and dry-lab bioinformatic mining — are getting better every year.
The CRISPR systems and novel antibiotics we've already gotten are early returns from a library we've barely begun to explore.
There is, almost certainly, much more.
The limitations are real.
Many environmental gene clusters don't express in standard heterologous hosts because they need specific regulatory cues or precursors.
Bioinformatic predictions of biosynthetic clusters often turn out to be silent or to produce known compounds rather than novel ones.
The hit rate per attempt is low.
But the absolute number of attempts you can make scales with sequencing capacity, which keeps falling in cost.
So even with low hit rates, the throughput grows exponentially, and the discovery pipeline keeps producing.
This is, in the long view, maybe the most underappreciated payoff of the metagenomics era.
The microbiome era didn't just give us new tools to study humans.
It gave us access to a vast, ancient, uncultured chemical and genetic reservoir, and we're slowly learning how to mine it.
